coredata.dc:title: Surface state dominated and carbon core coordinated red-emitting carbon dots for the detection of Cr2O7 2- and cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Pengju
Liu, Baoqiang
Duan, Jinjing
Wei, Shanshan
Zhang, Hongyuan
Wang, Jiali
Guo, Hui
Guo, Yidan
Jiang, Chunzhu
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: Cr(VI) as a toxic heavy metal ion can easily enter into the body through drinking or eating and cause liver and kidney diseases as well as cancer. Considering its high biological toxicity and adverse effects on human body, it is desirable to develop a probe to monitor its level in the environment. Herein, a high-efficiency fluorescent nanoprobe based on red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) was established through a convenient solvothermal strategy. The as-prepared CDs with excitation-independency had the fixed emission wavelength at 627 nm when the excitation wavelength was 560 nm. Further study manifested that the new surface state formed by nitrogen and sulfur doping and the increased conjugated system established through dehydration and carbonization were the main reasons for the fluorescence redshift. In this system, these R-CDs as a fluorescent probe exhibited high specificity and sensitivity to Cr2O7 2- with the linear range of 4–40 μΜ and the limit of detection could reach 80.00 nM. The quenching of these CDs by Cr2O7 2- was efficiently induced through a static quenching process. Meanwhile, the obtained CDs could enter into HeLa cells through endocytosis and exhibit bright red fluorescence in cells under a confocal laser scanning microscope. Thus, this work provided a promising probe not only for detecting Cr(VI) in natural environment but also for imaging in cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cr2O7 2-
N/S-doping
Surface state
Red emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of R-CDs toward Cr2O7 2-

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Project (20170101094JC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HR-TEM image, (c) XRD pattern, (d) Raman spectrum of these R-CDs.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of the obtained R-CDs. (b)The XPS full spectrum of the obtained R-CDs. (c, d, e, f) The XPS spectra and fitting gaussian peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p.

(a) The UV–vis spectrum of these R-CDs (inset: the R-CDs solution under daylight and UV light). (b) The fluorescence emission spectra of these R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Cyclic voltammogram. (d) The decay curve of the R-CDs. (e) Energy level simulation.

(a) The influence of pH on R-CDs. (b) The selectivity of R-CDs towards Cr2O7 2-. (c)The fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of Cr2O7 2- (4–40 μM). (d) The linear relationship between FL and Cr2O7 2- concentration.

(a) Cell viability with different concentrations of R-CDs for 24 h. Confocal images of HeLa cells cultivated with R-CDs (100 μg·mL−1) (b) under bright field, (c) under dark field. (d) The merged image.
The luminescence mechanism of the red-emitting CDs and these R-CDs as a highly efficient “on–off” fluorescent sensor for detecting Cr2O7 2- in environment and imaging in the living-cells.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of Cr2O7 2-.
Recovery of Cr2O7 2- in actual sewage water samples.

head.ce:title: Surface state dominated and carbon core coordinated red-emitting carbon dots for the detection of Cr2O7 2- and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012. PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012. PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 The luminescence mechanism of the red-emitting CDs and R-CDs as a highly efficient “on–off” fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr2O7 2- and imaging in the living-cells.

Cr(VI) as a toxic heavy metal ion can easily enter into the body through drinking or eating and cause liver and kidney diseases as well as cancer. Considering its high biological toxicity and adverse effects on human body, it is desirable to develop a probe to monitor its level in the environment. Herein, a high-efficiency fluorescent nanoprobe based on red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) was established through a convenient solvothermal strategy. The as-prepared CDs with excitation-independency had the fixed emission wavelength at 627 nm when the excitation wavelength was 560 nm. Further study manifested that the new surface state formed by nitrogen and sulfur doping and the increased conjugated system established through dehydration and carbonization were the main reasons for the fluorescence redshift. In this system, these R-CDs as a fluorescent probe exhibited high specificity and sensitivity to Cr2O7 2- with the linear range of 4–40 μΜ and the limit of detection could reach 80.00 nM. The quenching of these CDs by Cr2O7 2- was efficiently induced through a static quenching process. Meanwhile, the obtained CDs could enter into HeLa cells through endocytosis and exhibit bright red fluorescence in cells under a confocal laser scanning microscope. Thus, this work provided a promising probe not only for detecting Cr(VI) in natural environment but also for imaging in cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) and its compounds have been widely used in electroplating, metal processing, leather making and dye industry due to their high hardness, wear resistance, flexibility, strong coloring power and excellent permeability [1–3]. Usually, these chromium compounds are released into the environment in the form of dichromate (Cr2O7 2-) through waste disposal and factory effluent, resulting in the environment pollution. What's worse, this oxide is considered as the number one pollutant discharged from industrial wastewater and cannot be degraded under natural conditions. The ingestion of a small amount Cr2O7 2- may lead to skin diseases and nasal ulcers, while accumulated a large amount of the oxide in the body can destroy proteins, enzymes and DNA, causing liver damage, acute kidney injury and malignant tumors [4–6]. For protecting humans from Cr(VI) pollution, the World Health Organization (WHO) sets a threshold of 0.8 µM for Cr2O7 2- in drinking-water [7]. Therefore, the quick and trace monitoring of Cr(VI) can effectively promote pollution control as well as reduce the risk of excessive exposure to Cr(VI).

Currently, the methods for detecting Cr2O7 2- mainly rely on laboratory analysis techniques, for instance, ion chromatography (IC), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and so on [8–11]. These detection methods require specialized and costly equipment, complicated detection process and relatively long measurement time. Contrast to the above-mentioned analytical methods, fluorescence spectrometry can provide a faster and more convenient strategy for the detection of trace analytes through fluorescence enhancement or quenching. The nanomaterials used in fluorescence spectrometry include carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, nanoparticles based on organic fluorescent dyes, rare earth-doped up conversion luminescent nanomaterials, and semiconductor quantum dots, etc [12,13]. As a new class of carbon-based fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) have received vast notice in sensing, catalysis, and biomedicine due to the preponderances of excellent optical property, low toxicity, and primitive synthetic strategy. Wang et al. exploited the fluorescent carbon dots (FCD) with high solubility in water, which showed high selectivity for Cr(Ⅵ) with an LOD of 0.56 mg·mL−1 [14]. Qiao utilized a one-pot ultrasonic irradiation method to produce bright blue emission CDs and the fluorescence of CDs was remarkably quenched by Cr2O7 2− with the LOD of 140 nM [15]. Singh synthesized algal derived nitrogen phosphorous carbon dots (A-NPCDs) for detecting Hg2+ and Cr(Ⅵ) with the LOD up to 0.018 μM [16]. Although these CDs can detect the ion in a simple approach, the problems of unsatisfactory LOD and poor specific recognition ability still exist. Therefore, it is extremely essential to develop a CDs-based fluorescent probe with a simple preparation method, realizing highly sensitive and selective detection of trace amounts Cr(Ⅵ).

Usually, CDs emit visible light in the blue-green light region, which limits the application of these CDs in the field of biological imaging due to the self-fluorescence phenomenon of organism and tissues. In contrast, red emission CDs have stronger tissue penetration and lower tissue damage, which are more conducive to applicate in the biological field [17–20]. Shi et al. developed red-emitting fluorescent CDs using p-phenylenediamine and ethanol as raw materials through solvothermal strategy and column chromatography purification, which had dual targeting property and were applied in polar imaging in living cells [21]. Feng and his colleagues prepared red-emission CDs with hydrophobic porphyrin structure for cell imaging by magnetic stirring at room temperature, but the synthesis time was as long as 120 h [19]. Although many breakthroughs have been made for the preparation of red-emitting CDs, the preparation methods also had some disadvantages such as complex synthesis process, long preparation cycles or cumbersome post-processing. Therefore, the bottleneck in the preparation of red-emitting CDs is necessary to be explored.

On the basis of the above considerations, a kind of novel red emissive CDs (R-CDs) was prepared by a facile solvothermal strategy utilizing o-PDA and p-ABSA as precursors (Scheme 1 ). It has been demonstrated that the synergistic effect of the new surface state and the large sp2 domain led to the red emitting of these CDs. The obtained CDs exhibited sensitive and speedy fluorescence response to Cr2O7 2- by forming hydrogen bonds between as-prepared R-CDs and Cr2O7 2-. Moreover, the excellent fluorescence stability and great water solubility of these R-CDs along with its high recovery in standard samples made them possible to detect Cr2O7 2- in real environment. In addition, their long-wavelength emission, relatively high QY, and low toxicity developed their application in the field of bio-imaging.

The high-efficiency R-CDs were prepared using o-PDA and p-ABSA as precursors by a simple solvothermal strategy. Briefly, 1 mmol o-PDA, 1 mmol p-ABSA, 11 mL deionized water and 9 mL sulfuric acid were sequentially added to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and reacted at 200 °C under autogenous pressure for 3 h (Fig. S1). The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and purified by dialysis (MWCO = 100 Da) for 12 h. Ultimately, the black powdery CDs were collected by freeze-drying and stored at 4 ℃ for further experiments.

A certain amount of K2Cr2O7 was dissolved in deionized water to prepare the stock solution. The sensitivity of these R-CDs to Cr2O7 2- (100 µL, 4–40 μM) at different volumes (40 µL, 50 µL, 60 µL) was evaluated by calculating correlation coefficients (Fig. S1 e, f). The fluorescence (FL) intensity of obtained R-CDs (50 µL, 1.0 mg·mL−1) with Cr2O7 2- (100 µL, 30 µM) was recorded under different pH to select the optimal pH value. Subsequently, the optimal incubation time was specified in the same way. Fluorescence emission spectra were all recorded at 560 nm excitation wavelength.

To eliminate the interference of Fe3+ and MnO4 -, the solutions of H2O2 (50 μL, 30 %) and NaF (50 μL, 0.1 M) as masking agents were separately added to 50 μL the R-CDs. Subsequently, Fe3+, MnO4 -, and Cr2O7 2- (100 μL, 40 μM) solutions were added to centrifuge tube with the above mixture and then diluted with deionized water to 3 mL.

These R-CDs were applied to detect Cr2O7 2- in sewage samples. The real sewage samples from the Changchun Sewage Treatment Plant were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to move away the insoluble substances for subsequently use. Next, various solutions of Cr2O7 2- with different concentrations (10 µM, 18 µM, 36 µM) were added to the above solutions to simulate the real contaminated samples. 100 µL sewage sample was added to our prepared R-CDs solution (1.0 mg·mL−1, 50 µL), then diluted to 3 mL and incubated for 1 min. Finally, the FL intensity of this solution was recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer.

To assess the biological safety and expand the application of as-prepared R-CDs in the biological direction, the cell toxicity of these R-CDs was evaluated by the MTT experiment. Briefly, the HeLa cells in 96-well plate were first cultivated in a culture medium involving 1 % penicillin, 1 % streptomycin, and 10 % fetal bovine serum under a 5 % CO2 environment at 37 °C. After 24 h incubation, the old supernatant was removed and the cells were washed three times with sterile PBS buffer, then these R-CDs (0–1000 µg·mL−1) with DMEM were dropwise added into the 96-well plate and cultivated for one day. Whereafter, MTT reagent (0.50 mg·mL−1, 100 µL) was added to each well and cultivated for 4 h. After removing MTT reagent and adding DMSO into each well, the absorbance was measured at 490 nm using a microplate reader and the cell viability was assessed by calculating the ratio of the absorption intensity of the treated cells to the control cells:
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y =
A
S a m p l e
A
C o n t r o l
× 100 %

The imaging capabilities of obtained R-CDs in living cells were evaluated by HeLa cells. In a nutshell, HeLa cells were cultivated at 37 °C with 1 % streptomycin, 1 % penicillin and 10 % fetal bovine serum under a 5 % CO2 environment for one day. After discarding the supernatant, the cells were incubated in the medium supplemented with these R-CDs (100 µL, 800 µg·mL−1) for 12 h, and then gently rinsed with PBS for three times. Finally, bio-images of these treated cells were observed by an upright FV1000-D CLSM (Olympus).

The TEM image displayed that these red-emitting R-CDs prepared by a convenient solvothermal method had excellent monodispersity and spherical nanostructure with an average particle size of 2.43 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. 1 a). The HR-TEM image revealed that these R-CDs had an evident lattice fringe and the average in-plane crystal spacing was 0.32 nm, matching with the (002) diffraction facet of graphite (Fig. 1b) [22]. XRD pattern of these CDs displayed a broad diffraction peak at 23°, which could be attributed to (002) plane of graphitic carbon with the lattice spacing d = 0.34 nm, very near to 0.32 nm lattice spacing obtained by HR-TEM (Fig. 1c) [23–26]. In confocal Raman spectroscopy, the two peaks (1382 cm−1, 1631 cm−1) were assigned to D band and G band of graphite (Fig. 1d) [27]. The D band was corresponded to the defects in the graphene edges and surfaces causing by incorporation of heteroatoms. The G band of these CDs was related with the in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms inside the aromatic domain. The G band of these R-CDs slightly shifted to the blue wavelength in contrast to the emblematical in-plane vibration of the carbon at 1580 cm -1 (G band) due to the presence of highly N/S-doping (N, 11.52 % and S, 2.5 %) [28]. All these results fully certified that these R-CDs possessed certainly graphitized structure and excellent crystallization performance.

The organo-functional groups on the surface of as-prepared R-CDs were investigated by the FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a). The broad absorption band around 3369 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, and the CH stretching vibrations of aromatic rings and alkyl groups were located around 3000 cm−1 [29]. The peak at 2628 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching modes of SH. And the peak of CO/CN was observed at 1624 cm−1, manifesting the existence of groups containing N/O in the graphite [30–32]. The stretching vibration of benzene ring framework existed around 1448 cm−1 to 1512 cm−1. The peak at the wavenumber of 1380 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN and the deformation vibration of OH [33]. The peaks at approximately 1078 cm−1 and 669 cm−1 were identified as the -SO3 - group and CS bond [31,34]. These results indicated that a great deal of hydrophilic functional groups with N/S elements existed on the surface of the as-obtained R-CDs, ensuring good water solubility.

The element compositions of the obtained R-CDs were determined on XPS spectra, and the atomic percentages of C, O, N, and S were 62.20 %, 11.52 %, 23.78 %, and 2.5 %, respectively (Fig. 2b). The C 1 s spectrum was divided into three different carbon states (283.9 eV, 284.4 eV, and 286.1 eV), assigning to CC/CC, CN/CO and CS/CO/CN [21,35,36], respectively (Fig. 2c). The two different peaks in the N 1 s spectrum were designated to graphitic N (401.9 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.9 eV) (Fig. 2d) [22,37]. It illustrated that the N element existed both in the carbon skeleton and on the surface of R-CDs. The two different peaks (532 eV and 533 eV) were attributed to CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2e) [36,38]. Furthermore, the spectrum of S 2p was split into two peaks (168.63 eV and 169.7 eV), relating to C-SOx- -C (x = 2, 3) (Fig. 2f) [39–41]. The above results confirmed that these red-emitting fluorescent CDs with graphitic N doped into the framework and surface-embedded functional groups containing N, S, and O elements were formed through dehydration and carbonization.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of these R-CDs were collected to estimate the optical features of the R-CDs. Two characteristic absorption bands at 230–300 nm and 480–600 nm in UV–vis absorption spectrum could be accredited to the electronic transition of CC and CN/CO (Fig. 3 a) [35]. These R-CDs solution exhibited blue-green color in sunlight, but it emitted bright red fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 3a insert). Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectrum shown that the R-CDs with excitation-independency had the emission peak centered at 627 nm when excitation wavelength was changed from 480 nm to 570 nm (Fig. 3b). This fluorescence behavior suggested that the surface of these R-CDs was almost uniform with similar band gaps [42]. The average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was 0.53 ns recorded by static fluorescence spectrometer. The fluorescence quantum yield of the R-CDs measured by integrating sphere was 8.15 %, which was very favorable for cell-imaging.

The fluorescence stability was explored before investigating the application of these CDs in ion detection and cell imaging. The FL intensities of these R-CDs hardly changed in NaCl solution with different concentrations (0–10-1 M) (Fig. S2a). The FL intensities of the R-CDs remained invariant after being irradiated by ultraviolet light for 3 h, which revealed that these CDs had strong resistance to photobleaching (Fig. S2b). In strong acid and base solutions (pH = 1.0, 2.0, 13.0, 14.0), the fluorescence of these R-CDs was quenched by the protonation and deprotonation of organic functional groups (–NH2/-SH/–COOH) of the obtained R-CDs. However, the FL intensity of these R-CDs was stable in the range of pH = 3.0–12.0, suggesting that the R-CDs had widely used in real environment detection (Fig. S2c). After incubation with H2O2 (100 μL, 30 %) for 10 min, the FL intensity remained almost stable, which indicated that the as-prepared R-CDs had strong oxidation resistance (Fig. S2d). Furthermore, the obtained R-CDs solution did not precipitate at room temperature after 5 months. In summary, this stable optical performance made these R-CDs have potential application value in the field of biological imaging.

So far, the luminescence mechanism of CDs mainly includes intrinsic state/carbon nucleus luminescence, molecular luminescence, surface state/edge excessive luminescence and coordinated effect of multiple luminescence centers [43–45]. The red emission mechanism of the obtained R-CDs was explored througth the UV–vis spectrum, FT-IR spectrum, Raman spectrum, XRD, fluorescence lifetime fitting equation and energy level simulation. The results of TEM, XRD and Raman demonstrated that the R-CDs had an excellent graphitization structure with large conjugate domain, which could lead to a decrease in the transition energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest empty molecular orbital (LUMO). Meanwhile, FT-IR and XPS spectra exposed that the edges and surfaces of these R-CDs were embedded with many functional groups (–COOH/–NH2/-SH). Thereby, a new LUMO energy level was formed, which could decrease the band gap between HOMO and LUMO due to the special edge structure. To ascertain the energy level, the following formulas were emploied to estimate HOMO and LUMO energies of the as- prepared CDs.
(1)
E
LUMO
= - e
E
Red
+ 4.4
(2)
E
HOMO
=
E
LUMO
-
E g

Herein, ERed represented the reduction potential of the as-obtained R-CDs and was measured to be 0.39 eV by cyclic voltammetry. Then, the energy level of LUMO (ELUMO ) was calculated to be −4.01 eV according to formula (1) (Fig. 3c). From formula (2), the EHOMO value was estimated to be −6.22 eV, here Eg was the band gap (2.21 eV) obtained from the absorption edge in the UV–vis spectrum [35]. The calculation results illustrated that these R-CDs had higher HOMO energy and lower LUMO energy, which made the electronic transition easy and the fluorescence emission wavelength move to longer wavelengths.

To further explore the main emission centers of the R-CDs, time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy was utilized to record the decay curve of these R-CDs and the transition form of electrons from the ground states to the excited states was analyzed (Fig. 3d). As expected, the curve conformed to the double exponential fitting, suggestting that there were two or more fluorescence centers in these CDs. According to the fluorescence lifetime fitting equation (
I
t
=
I 0
+
A 1
e
( - t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e
( - t /
τ
2 )
), there were two possible emission centers (the carbon core state and the surface state) in the R-CDs. Herein, τ1/τ2 symoblize the lifetimes of the molecule in the excited states S1 and S2, respectively. A1/A2 represente the probability of radiative transition from the two excited states (Table S1). A2 > A1 manifested that the surface state luminescence was dominant in the two luminescence centers of these R-CDs and the carbon core state was auxiliary luminescence [46,47].

In summary, the luminescence mechanism of these R-CDs could be attributed to the following facts. On the one hand, the formation of a larger conjugation domain in the carbon core structure reduced the band gap between HOMO and LUMO orbitals of these R-CDs. On the other hand, the surface and edge of these R-CDs were modified by a large number of functional groups to form a new surface state, which led to a further decrease in the band gap (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of R-CDs toward Cr2O7 2-
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of reaction time and pH value on the system were discussed to improve the sensitivity of these R-CDs. After the R-CDs (50 µL, 1 mg·mL−1) were incubated with Cr2O7 2- (100 µL, 30 µM) for 1 min, the quenching effect achieved 95 % with slight fluctuation as time increased to 10 min (Fig. S3a). The fluorescence stability of R-CDs/Cr2O7 2- system was detected in the stable pH range (3.0–12.0) of as-obtained R-CDs (Fig. 4 a). And the fluorescence intensity of this system did not change obviously in the pH range of the industrial sewage discharge standard (6.0–9.0). To simplify the experiment, the neutral condition pH = 7.0 was chosen as the subsequent analysis condition. The optimal experimental conditions (pH, 7.0 and incubation time, 1 min) were selected for the follow-up experiments. To evaluate the specific selection of the R-CDs to Cr2O7 2-, potential contending ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cr2O7 2-, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, MnO4 -, ClO-) and small molecules (Arg, Cys, His and Glu) were surveyed under the above conditions (Fig. 4b). 100 µL above ions (0.1 M) and small biomolecules were instilled into 50 µL R-CDs (1.0 mg·mL−1) solution, respectively. The FL intensity of these R-CDs did not display significant change except for Cr2O7 2-, MnO4 -, and Fe3+. Fortunately, these false positive signals of MnO4 - and Fe3+ could be effectively avoided by adding H2O2 and NaF [48]. The fluorescence intensities of the R-CDs solution containing masking agents remained basically unchanged after adding Fe3+ and MnO4 - but was significantly quenched after adding Cr2O7 2- (Fig. S3b). With the increased concentrations of Cr2O7 2- (100 µL, 4–40 µM), the FL intensity of as-prepared R-CDs (50 µL, 1 mg·mL−1) at 627 nm declined gradually and the maximum quenching efficiency reached 88 % after adding 40 µM Cr2O7 2- (Fig. 4c). Moreover, there existed a brilliant linear relationship between FL (where FL was the FL intensities of the solution containing R-CDs and Cr2O7 2-) and Cr2O7 2- concentration with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997 (Fig. 4d). The LOD of Cr2O7 2- was 80 nM on the basis of the formula of LOD = 3σ/K (σ is the standard deviation of blank, and K resembles the slope after calibration plot), which was significantly lower than previously reported values (Table 1 ). Therefore, these CDs could provide the possibility for economic and effective detection of Cr2O7 2- in nature environment with a wider linear range and a lower limit of detection.

The fluorescence lifetime, FT-IR spectra and UV–vis spectra of the R-CDs were measured to ascertain the mechanism behind the observed fluorescence quenching of these R-CDs. The fluorescence lifetimes of the obtained R-CDs recorded by time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy remained basically unchanged before and after dripping Cr2O7 2- (Fig. S3c). Thus, Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching mechanism were excluded, and the possible mechanism was static quenching or internal filtering effect (IFE). Then, the obvious absorption of Cr2O7 2- (red line) at 360 nm in the UV–vis spectrum did not correspond to the fluorescence excitation or emission position of the R-CDs. The IFE effect between R-CDs and Cr2O7 2- was ruled out (Fig. S3d). The electron-rich oxygen atoms in Cr2O7 2- could damage the surface passivation layer of the R-CDs by forming intermolecular X − H∙∙∙O bond [56]. Further, a new obvious absorption peak (348 nm) appeared at the UV-absorption curves of the mixture containing R-CDs and Cr2O7 2-, the stretching vibrations of OH, NH bonds of carboxyl group and amide disappeared after adding Cr2O7 2- in FT-IR spectrum. These results manifested that hydrogen bonds were formed between these CDs and Cr2O7 2-, so the fluorescence quenching of the R-CDs through the static quenching process [6,47].

The detection ability of the probe with the existence of interfering substances was evaluated by detecting Cr2O7 2- in actual sewage samples. An appropriate amount of K2Cr2O7 was dispersed into sewage samples to prepare spiked samples containing different concentrations of Cr2O7 2- (10 μM, 18 μM, 36 μM). Whereafter, 50 µL R-CDs solution and 100 µL spiked sample were mixed and diluted to 3 mL, after incubating for 1 min, the FL intensities of the mixtures were recorded by fluorescence spectrometer under 560 nm excitation wavelength. The recoveries of Cr2O7 2- in sewage water were 103.0 %, 96.15 %, and 99.42 % with the relative standard deviations between 1.73 % and 7.22 %, indicating that this method had high accuracy and reliability (Table 2 ). Therefore, these R-CDs might be utilized as a convenient and efficient probe to detect Cr2O7 2- in the actual environment.

To assess the feasibleness of these R-CDs in cell-imaging, the MTT method was utilized to evaluate the cytotoxicity of obtained R-CDs. The living HeLa cells were co-incubated with the R-CDs solution at various concentrations (0–1000 μg·mL−1) for one day. Satisfactory, the cell viability remained basically unchanged when the concentration of these R-CDs was less than 200 μg·mL−1 and the cell survival rate maintained 86 % as the concentration of these R-CDs increased up to 1000 μg·mL−1 (Fig. 5 a). These data manifested that the R-CDs exhibited negligible cytotoxicity and potential application in bio-imaging.

Inspired by the above results, HeLa cells were explored as biological models to assess the latent imaging capability of the R-CDs. The cells incubated with these R-CDs did not display fluorescence in the bright field image (Fig. 5b). The cytoplasm of the HeLa cells displayed clear and bright red intracellular fluorescence, suggesting that the R-CDs could pass through the cell membrane (Fig. 5c). After co-incubation with these R-CDs, these cells still maintained intact morphology seen from the merge image (Fig. 5d). As expected, the obtained R-CDs with the small particle size had the capability of entering into cells successful through endocytosis, which could broaden the application prospects of these R-CDs in the field of bio-imaging.

In this study, the surface state dominated and carbon core coordinated red-emitting CDs were prepared by a simple solvothermal process for detecting Cr2O7 2- and live-cell imaging. Both the new surface state doped by N/S and the large conjugate domain formed by carbonization could reduce the HOMO-LUMO energy level difference of the R-CDs, which made the radiative transition of electrons become easy and resulted in a red-shifted fluorescence emission. These R-CDs had been successfully applied to detect Cr2O7 2- in sewage with high sensitivity and specificity through the formation of hydrogen bond between the functional groups of these R-CDs (–NH2/–COOH/-SH) and Cr2O7 2-. Interestingly, owing to their brilliant water solubility and small size, these R-CDs could penetrate the cell membrane and translocate into cells by endocytosis. Therefore, the as-prepared red emission CDs could serve as a suitable sensor for detection of Cr(VI) in nature environmental and the potential application in the bio-imaging.

Pengju Lu: Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Baoqiang Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jinjing Duan: Supervision. Shanshan Wei: Writing – review & editing. Hongyuan Zhang: Software, Supervision. Jiali Wang: Conceptualization. Hui Guo: Writing – review & editing. Yidan Guo: Conceptualization, Supervision. Chunzhu Jiang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Guoying Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Project (20170101094JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121656.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
